enrichment.short_description: CoConstruct is construction project management software built for custom home builders and remodelers, now transitioning into Buildertrend following their 2021 merger.
enrichment.starting_price: $199/month (legacy plans; new customers are directed to Buildertrend)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: CoConstruct is construction management software for custom home builders, now transitioning to Buildertrend. See pricing, features, and migration details.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CoConstruct is a construction project management platform built specifically for custom home builders and remodelers, founded in 2005 and headquartered in Charlottesville, Virginia. It grew to serve more than 100,000 building professionals, with a typical customer base of firms building 5 to 50 homes per year. Core functionality historically included scheduling, budgeting and job costing, selections management (letting homeowners choose finishes and fixtures within a budget), change order tracking, client and subcontractor communication tools, photo and document storage, and a client portal that gives homeowners visibility into their project's progress. CoConstruct integrated with QuickBooks and Xero for accounting sync and offered mobile apps so site managers could update schedules and log information from job sites. On February 24, 2021, Buildertrend, a competing construction management platform, acquired CoConstruct in a deal backed by Bain Capital Tech Opportunities and HGGC, creating what was described at the time as the market leader in construction management software for residential builders. For several years after the acquisition, CoConstruct continued operating as a distinct product alongside Buildertrend. As of 2026, the two companies are actively merging their platforms: CoConstruct's own migration materials describe a phased move of existing customers onto Buildertrend, with historical CoConstruct project data remaining accessible indefinitely, but new projects only being creatable within CoConstruct through March 31, 2027, after which all new work must be started directly in Buildertrend. Reporting from third-party reviewers indicates CoConstruct has not received meaningful product updates since the acquisition and that Buildertrend is not investing further in its independent development, positioning it as a legacy product being wound down in favor of a unified Buildertrend platform. Historical CoConstruct pricing was structured in tiers (commonly cited as roughly $199/month for a small-team Core tier, $399/month for a mid-size Essential tier, and $599/month for an unlimited-user Advanced tier), billed based on active job sites with client access rather than per generic seat, with discounts for annual billing. Because it is being sunset in favor of Buildertrend, CoConstruct is not a viable option for a builder evaluating new software today; it remains relevant primarily as context for existing customers going through migration, or for understanding the construction management software market's consolidation. Buildertrend itself continues to be sold to new customers and now carries forward CoConstruct's client-communication and selections-management concepts within its own interface. Anyone currently on CoConstruct should expect ongoing but non-expanding support, continued access to legacy data, and an eventual full transition to Buildertrend's tooling and pricing structure rather than CoConstruct's original plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CoConstruct is a construction project management platform launched in 2005 in Charlottesville, Virginia, built specifically for custom home builders and remodeling contractors managing a handful of active projects at a time rather than large commercial sites.

In February 2021, competitor Buildertrend acquired CoConstruct, and the two companies have since been merging their platforms. As of 2026, CoConstruct is in an active wind-down: existing customers can continue using it and their historical data remains accessible, but new projects can only be started in CoConstruct through March 31, 2027, after which all new work moves to Buildertrend.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: CoConstruct's core toolset was built around the custom-build client experience: selections management for choosing finishes within budget, change order workflows, scheduling, and a client portal that keeps homeowners updated without constant phone calls.

On the operations side, it offered budgeting and job costing, subcontractor and vendor communication, photo and document storage for job sites, and accounting sync with QuickBooks and Xero, along with mobile access for site managers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: CoConstruct historically sold three tiers priced by active job sites with client access rather than flat per-seat pricing: a Core tier around $199/month for small teams, an Essential tier around $399/month, and an unlimited-user Advanced tier around $599/month, with a discount for annual billing.
Because CoConstruct is being folded into Buildertrend, these legacy prices are not being offered to new customers; prospective buyers are directed to Buildertrend's own pricing and plans instead.

features.title: Selections management
features.description: Lets homeowners choose finishes and fixtures within a defined budget, tracking allowances and overages.

features.title: Change order workflow
features.description: Structured process for proposing, approving, and billing changes to the original contract scope.

features.title: Client portal
features.description: Gives homeowners a single place to see schedules, photos, selections, and communication history.

features.title: Scheduling
features.description: Job-site scheduling tools for coordinating subcontractors and internal teams across multiple active projects.

features.title: Budgeting and job costing
features.description: Tracks estimated versus actual costs per project to help builders protect margins.

features.title: Accounting integrations
features.description: Syncs with QuickBooks and Xero to keep project financials aligned with company books.

features.title: Mobile access
features.description: Mobile apps let site managers update schedules, log photos, and communicate from the job site.

pros: Purpose-built for the custom home and remodeling workflow, including selections and change orders
Strong client communication tools that reduce phone and email back-and-forth with homeowners
Established integrations with common accounting software
Large existing user base with over 100,000 building professionals historically on the platform
cons: No longer actively developed as an independent product; the company is directing customers to Buildertrend
New projects cannot be created in CoConstruct after March 31, 2027
Not a realistic option for evaluating new software today given its planned sunset
Support and updates have reportedly slowed since the Buildertrend acquisition

faqs.question: Is CoConstruct still available for new customers?
faqs.answer: CoConstruct is not being actively promoted to new customers; Buildertrend, which acquired CoConstruct in 2021, is now the primary product being sold, with CoConstruct customers being migrated over on a phased timeline.

faqs.question: What happens to my existing CoConstruct data?
faqs.answer: According to CoConstruct's own migration guidance, historical project data remains accessible indefinitely even after a customer moves to Buildertrend.

faqs.question: Until when can I create new projects in CoConstruct?
faqs.answer: CoConstruct's migration page states that new projects can still be added through March 31, 2027, after which new projects must be created directly in Buildertrend.

faqs.question: What is the relationship between CoConstruct and Buildertrend?
faqs.answer: Buildertrend acquired CoConstruct in February 2021. The two were run as separate products for several years, but they are now merging into a single Buildertrend platform.

faqs.question: What did CoConstruct cost?
faqs.answer: Historical pricing was tiered by active job sites with client access, commonly cited around $199 to $599 per month depending on team size and features, though CoConstruct is no longer the primary product being sold to new buyers.

use_cases.title: Custom home builder managing client selections
use_cases.description: A builder running several custom homes at once uses selections management and the client portal to keep homeowners informed about finish choices and budget impact.

use_cases.title: Remodeling contractor tracking change orders
use_cases.description: A remodeling firm uses the change order workflow to document scope changes and get client sign-off before billing for extra work.

enrichment.short_description: Coda is an all-in-one docs, tables, and automation workspace, acquired by Grammarly in 2024 and rebranded to Superhuman Docs in July 2026.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Coda, now Superhuman Docs, blends documents, spreadsheets, and automation in one workspace. Compare its pricing, features, and 2026 Grammarly rebrand.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Coda is an all-in-one workspace that combines documents, spreadsheet-style tables, and app-like building blocks, originally founded in 2014 by Shishir Mehrotra along with Rehman Ali, Jean-Baptiste Coger, and Alex DeNeui, and headquartered in San Francisco. Coda's product model lets teams write free-form documents that also contain structured, relational tables, and layer in buttons, automations, and third-party integrations called Packs to turn a document into a lightweight application, positioning it as a hybrid between Google Docs, Airtable, and project management tools like Asana. On December 19, 2024, writing-assistant company Grammarly announced it was acquiring Coda, with Coda's CEO and co-founder Shishir Mehrotra becoming Grammarly's new CEO. In 2025, Grammarly separately acquired the AI email client Superhuman, founded by Rahul Vohra. On October 29, 2025, Grammarly announced a full company rebrand: the parent company itself became Superhuman, uniting Grammarly's writing assistant, Coda's workspace, and Superhuman's email client under one brand alongside a new AI assistant called Superhuman Go. As the final step of that transition, on July 8, 2026, Coda was renamed Superhuman Docs. According to Superhuman's own help documentation, existing Coda users keep all of their docs, tables, Packs, automations, and formulas working exactly as before under the new name, and the underlying product experience is unchanged aside from new AI capabilities built into documents and updated backend data infrastructure. Coda's pricing structure has continued largely unchanged through the rebrand: a Free plan caps shared docs at roughly 50 objects and 1,000 rows with basic collaboration; Pro costs $12 per Doc Maker per month ($10 if billed annually) and adds cross-doc tables and automations; Team costs $36 per Doc Maker per month ($30 annually) and adds administrative controls; and Enterprise is custom-priced with SSO/SCIM, one-year audit log retention, SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001/27017/27018 compliance, dedicated support, and a large monthly AI credit allowance per Doc Maker. A distinguishing part of Coda's billing model, which reportedly carries over to Superhuman Docs, is that only "Doc Makers" (people who create or manage docs) are billed; "Editors" who only fill in or edit existing content can collaborate at no charge. Coda/Superhuman Docs is available as a web app with native desktop apps for Mac and Windows and mobile apps for iOS and Android, and it exposes an API and a Packs SDK for building custom integrations. It's used by teams for internal wikis, product and project trackers, CRMs, OKR tracking, and other lightweight internal tools that would otherwise require separate spreadsheet and database software. Anyone researching or comparing this tool today should treat "Coda" and "Superhuman Docs" as the same underlying product under different names, with Superhuman Docs being the current, accurate branding as of mid-2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Coda launched in 2014 as an all-in-one document and database workspace, letting teams combine free-form writing with structured, relational tables and app-like automations in a single doc. Grammarly acquired Coda in December 2024, and in October 2025 Grammarly rebranded its whole company to Superhuman, bringing Coda into a unified product suite alongside Grammarly's writing assistant and the Superhuman email client.

On July 8, 2026, Coda itself was renamed Superhuman Docs. Existing docs, tables, Packs, automations, and formulas continue to work as before; the change is primarily branding plus new AI features and backend infrastructure, not a product replacement.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core of the product is its blend of free-form documents and structured tables: teams can write a project brief and embed a live, filterable database of tasks, contacts, or inventory in the same page, with changes reflected everywhere the data is referenced.

Packs (third-party integrations), buttons, and automations let a doc trigger actions like sending a Slack message, updating a calendar event, or pulling data from another tool, turning documents into lightweight internal applications. AI features are built directly into the writing and table-editing experience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pricing runs Free, Pro at $12 per Doc Maker per month ($10 billed annually), Team at $36 per Doc Maker per month ($30 billed annually), and custom-priced Enterprise. Only people who create or manage docs ("Doc Makers") are billed; collaborators who just edit content are free.
Enterprise adds SSO/SCIM, extended audit log retention, SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001/27017/27018 compliance, dedicated support, and a larger monthly AI credit allowance per Doc Maker.

features.title: Docs with embedded tables
features.description: Combine free-form text with structured, relational, spreadsheet-like tables in a single document.

features.title: Packs integrations
features.description: Connect to third-party tools (Slack, Jira, Google Calendar, and more) to pull in or act on external data.

features.title: Buttons and automations
features.description: Add clickable actions and scheduled automations that update rows, send notifications, or trigger workflows.

features.title: Cross-doc tables
features.description: Reference and sync tables across multiple docs, available on Pro and above plans.

features.title: AI in documents
features.description: Built-in AI assistance for writing, summarizing, and working with table data.

features.title: Doc Maker billing model
features.description: Only users who create/manage docs are billed; users who only edit existing content are free.

features.title: Cross-platform apps
features.description: Native apps for web, Mac, Windows, iOS, and Android keep docs accessible across devices.

pros: Flexible enough to replace multiple point tools (docs, spreadsheets, light project management) with one workspace
Doc Maker billing model can be cost-effective for teams with many read-only collaborators
Strong integration ecosystem via Packs
Existing docs and automations were preserved through the Grammarly acquisition and Superhuman Docs rebrand
cons: Recent rebrand to Superhuman Docs can create confusion when researching the product under its old "Coda" name
Per-Doc-Maker pricing can get expensive for organizations with many document creators
Advanced automation and cross-doc features are gated behind paid plans
Feature depth in any single area (docs, tables, automations) is generally less specialized than dedicated tools in that category

faqs.question: Is Coda still called Coda?
faqs.answer: No. As of July 8, 2026, Coda was renamed Superhuman Docs as part of Grammarly's broader rebrand of its parent company to Superhuman. It is the same underlying product.

faqs.question: What happens to my existing Coda docs after the rebrand?
faqs.answer: According to Superhuman's help documentation, existing docs, workflows, tables, Packs, automations, and formulas continue to work exactly as before under the new Superhuman Docs name.

faqs.question: Who owns Coda/Superhuman Docs now?
faqs.answer: Grammarly acquired Coda in December 2024. Grammarly itself rebranded to Superhuman in October 2025, and Coda's co-founder Shishir Mehrotra became CEO of the combined company.

faqs.question: How much does Superhuman Docs (Coda) cost?
faqs.answer: It has a free tier plus paid Pro ($12/Doc Maker/month) and Team ($36/Doc Maker/month) plans, with custom Enterprise pricing; only document creators, not editors, are billed.

faqs.question: Is there a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, native apps are available for iOS and Android in addition to web, Mac, and Windows apps.

use_cases.title: Internal team wiki with live data
use_cases.description: Teams build an internal knowledge base where policy docs sit alongside live, filterable tables of projects, contacts, or assets.

use_cases.title: Lightweight product/project tracker
use_cases.description: Product teams replace a mix of spreadsheets and task tools with a single doc containing a roadmap table, status buttons, and automations.
use_cases.audience: Product managers and small cross-functional teams

features: Up to ~50 objects and 1,000 rows per doc
12,000+ monthly AI credits per Doc Maker

enrichment.short_description: Devin Desktop is an AI-powered code editor, originally launched as Codeium in 2021, renamed Windsurf in 2024, and rebranded again to Devin Desktop by Cognition AI in June 2026.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Devin Desktop, formerly Codeium and then Windsurf, is an AI coding editor now under Cognition AI. See its 2026 rebrand, pricing, and features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: This tool has gone through three names in its history. It began in 2021 as Exafunction, a GPU virtualization startup founded by Varun Mohan and Douglas Chen and based in Mountain View, California. In 2022 the company pivoted to AI-assisted coding and rebranded as Codeium, an AI autocomplete and chat plugin for editors like VS Code and JetBrains IDEs, offering free-forever autocomplete as its core hook alongside paid tiers for teams. In late 2024, the company rebranded again to Windsurf and shipped Windsurf Editor, a full standalone AI-native IDE (a fork of VS Code) built around an agentic coding mode called Cascade, moving the product from a plugin-only tool to a complete development environment; the Codeium name was retained for the underlying plugin and account/authentication layer. Windsurf became one of the most closely watched AI coding startups of 2025, including a widely reported near-acquisition attempt by Google that instead resulted in Google licensing Windsurf's technology and hiring some of its leadership. In December 2025, Cognition AI, the developer of the autonomous coding agent Devin (founded in August 2023 by Scott Wu, Steven Hao, and Walden Yan, headquartered in San Francisco), acquired Windsurf for a reported price around $250 million. On June 2, 2026, Cognition rebranded Windsurf to Devin Desktop, folding it fully under the Devin product family. According to Cognition's documentation, this was a like-for-like rename delivered as an over-the-air update to existing installs: accounts, plans, extensions, keybindings, and MCP connections carried over automatically, and pricing was unchanged through the transition. Functionally, Devin Desktop replaced the home screen with an Agent Command Center, a Kanban-style board for managing local and cloud coding agents, and introduced Devin Local, a faster, Rust-based successor to Cascade (which reached end of life on July 1, 2026) capable of spawning parallel subagents. The editor also added support for the open Agent Client Protocol (ACP), letting third-party agents such as Codex, Claude Agent, and Gemini CLI run inside the same interface rather than being locked to one vendor's agent. As of mid-2026, pricing is Free (unlimited basic autocomplete, chat, and limited agent usage across 70+ programming languages), Pro at $20/month, Max at $200/month for power users, Teams at $80/month plus $40 per seat, and custom Enterprise pricing, including legacy Windsurf Enterprise contracts. Plugins remain available for VS Code, JetBrains IDEs, Neovim, Visual Studio, and other editors in addition to the full standalone desktop app, on macOS and Windows. Anyone researching this product under the name "Codeium" should understand that the current, accurate name as of mid-2026 is Devin Desktop, developed by Cognition AI.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: This AI coding tool has changed names twice since its 2021 founding as Exafunction. It became Codeium in 2022 as an AI autocomplete plugin, then Windsurf in late 2024 as a full standalone AI-native code editor built around an agentic mode called Cascade.

In December 2025, Cognition AI (maker of the Devin coding agent) acquired Windsurf, and on June 2, 2026 rebranded it to Devin Desktop. The rebrand was delivered as an update to existing installs, preserving accounts, plans, and configuration, with Devin Local replacing Cascade as the built-in agent.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The product combines traditional AI autocomplete and in-editor chat with agentic task execution: an agent can read a codebase, plan multi-step changes, edit multiple files, and run commands with developer oversight.

The Agent Command Center presents a Kanban-style view of local and cloud agents, and the editor supports the Agent Client Protocol (ACP), letting outside agents such as Codex, Claude Agent, and Gemini CLI run inside the same interface. Editor plugins remain available for VS Code, JetBrains IDEs, and other environments for developers who don't want the full standalone app.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Free plan offers unlimited basic autocomplete, in-editor chat, and limited agent usage across 70+ programming languages with no credit card required. Paid tiers are Pro at $20/month, Max at $200/month, and Teams at $80/month plus $40 per seat.
Enterprise pricing is custom and, per Cognition's documentation, legacy Windsurf Enterprise contracts continue to be honored under the Devin Desktop name.

features.title: AI autocomplete
features.description: Single- and multi-line code completions across 70+ programming languages in supported editors.

features.title: In-editor chat
features.description: Ask questions about the codebase or generate code directly inside the editor.

features.title: Devin Local (formerly Cascade)
features.description: Built-in agentic mode that can plan and execute multi-file code changes and run commands, rewritten in Rust for efficiency.

features.title: Agent Command Center
features.description: Kanban-style dashboard for tracking and managing multiple local and cloud coding agents at once.

features.title: Agent Client Protocol (ACP) support
features.description: Open protocol allowing third-party agents like Codex, Claude Agent, and Gemini CLI to run inside the editor.

features.title: Editor plugins
features.description: Plugins for VS Code, JetBrains IDEs, Neovim, and Visual Studio for developers who prefer their existing editor.

features.title: Spaces
features.description: Groups sessions, PRs, files, and Git worktrees so multiple agents can share context on related work.

pros: Generous free tier with unlimited basic autocomplete and support for 70+ languages
Backward-compatible rebrand meant existing Windsurf/Codeium users kept accounts, plans, and settings automatically
Open Agent Client Protocol support avoids locking users into a single vendor's agent
Backed by Cognition AI's broader Devin agent ecosystem and ongoing investment
cons: Two rebrands in under two years (Codeium to Windsurf to Devin Desktop) make the product harder to track and compare across reviews
Cascade, the previous built-in agent, was fully retired on July 1, 2026, requiring users to adapt to Devin Local
Pro plan pricing has increased over time (from $15 to $20/month as of mid-2026)
Full agentic feature set requires the standalone desktop app rather than the lightweight plugin

faqs.question: Is Codeium still called Codeium?
faqs.answer: No. Codeium became Windsurf in late 2024, and Windsurf was rebranded to Devin Desktop by Cognition AI on June 2, 2026. Codeium now refers to the underlying authentication and backend technology.

faqs.question: Do I need to reinstall anything after the rebrand?
faqs.answer: No. Cognition shipped Devin Desktop as an over-the-air update to existing Windsurf installs, carrying over accounts, plans, extensions, keybindings, and MCP connections.

faqs.question: What happened to Cascade?
faqs.answer: Cascade, Windsurf's original built-in agent, reached end of life on July 1, 2026, and was replaced by Devin Local, a Rust-based agent reported to be roughly 30% more token-efficient.

faqs.question: Did pricing change with the rebrand?
faqs.answer: No. Cognition's documentation states pricing is unchanged through the rebrand, including for legacy Windsurf Enterprise plans.

faqs.question: Who owns this product now?
faqs.answer: Cognition AI, the developer of the Devin autonomous coding agent, acquired Windsurf in December 2025 for a reported price of about $250 million.

use_cases.title: Agent-assisted feature development
use_cases.description: A developer describes a feature in natural language and Devin Local plans and implements multi-file changes with the developer reviewing each step.

use_cases.title: Lightweight autocomplete in an existing editor
use_cases.description: A developer who prefers VS Code or a JetBrains IDE installs the plugin for free AI autocomplete and chat without switching editors.

enrichment.short_description: Cognism is a B2B sales intelligence platform providing GDPR-compliant, phone-verified contact and company data for outbound sales teams, especially in EMEA.
enrichment.seo_title: Cognism: B2B Sales Intelligence Pricing & Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cognism provides GDPR-compliant, phone-verified B2B contact data for sales teams. See how it compares on pricing, coverage, and CRM integrations.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cognism is a B2B sales intelligence and contact data platform founded in 2015 (formally incorporated in 2017) by James Isilay, a former algorithmic trader, and Stjepan Buljat, a software architecture specialist. The company is headquartered in London, with additional offices in New York, Skopje, and Zadar, and reported around $83 million in annual recurring revenue with roughly 6,400 customers as of 2024, growing toward the $100 million ARR range by 2025. Cognism's core product delivers verified contact information (email addresses and, notably, human-verified mobile phone numbers) plus firmographic and intent data to power outbound sales prospecting, primarily for teams targeting Europe, the Middle East, and Africa (EMEA), where the company positions itself as having stronger coverage than US-centric competitors like ZoomInfo. Its Diamond Data tier uses human researchers who manually call and confirm mobile numbers rather than relying purely on algorithmic prediction, claiming around 87% accuracy, and the company maintains a database of more than 10 million phone-verified contacts. Cognism emphasizes regulatory compliance as a core differentiator: it is GDPR- and CCPA-compliant, holds ISO 27001 and SOC 2 Type II certifications, is registered with the UK Information Commissioner's Office, is registered as a data broker under CCPA in the United States, and cross-references contacts against Do Not Call and Telephone Preference Service lists across roughly 15 territories. The platform integrates with major CRM and sales engagement tools, including Salesforce, HubSpot, Outreach, Salesloft, Microsoft Dynamics 365, Bullhorn, and Pipedrive, with native connectors that carry compliance metadata along with the synced contact data, plus a Chrome extension that surfaces verified contact details while browsing LinkedIn or a target company's website. Cognism does not publish pricing publicly; prospective customers go through a sales demo and negotiation process. Third-party sources tracking real deal data (such as Vendr) report that actual Cognism contracts commonly range from roughly $1,500 to $25,000 per year depending on tier, team size, and negotiation, with named tiers historically including a Platinum/Grow tier starting around $1,500 per user per year and a Diamond/Elevate tier starting around $2,500 per user per year, on top of platform fees that can run $15,000 to $25,000 annually before per-seat costs. Because none of these figures come from Cognism's own published pricing page, they should be treated as directional estimates rather than confirmed rates. Cognism is best suited for outbound sales, SDR, and RevOps teams that need compliant, mobile-verified contact data for cold calling and email outreach, particularly those selling into European markets where data protection rules are stricter than in the US.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cognism is a B2B sales intelligence platform founded in 2015 by James Isilay and Stjepan Buljat and headquartered in London, with additional offices in New York, Skopje, and Zadar. It provides verified contact and company data to power outbound prospecting, with a particular strength in coverage across EMEA markets.

The company built its reputation on human-verified mobile phone data and strict regulatory compliance, positioning itself against US-centric providers like ZoomInfo for teams that need GDPR- and CCPA-compliant data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Cognism's Diamond Data uses human researchers to manually verify mobile numbers rather than relying solely on algorithmic prediction, and the platform maintains over 10 million phone-verified contacts globally alongside firmographic and intent data.

It integrates natively with Salesforce, HubSpot, Outreach, Salesloft, Microsoft Dynamics 365, Bullhorn, and Pipedrive, and offers a Chrome extension for pulling verified contact data while browsing LinkedIn or company websites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Cognism does not publish pricing publicly; buyers go through a demo and sales negotiation to get a quote. Third-party deal-tracking sources report typical real-world contracts ranging from around $1,500 to $25,000 per year depending on tier, seat count, and negotiation.
These figures are not confirmed by Cognism's own materials and should be treated as estimates; prospective buyers should request a current quote directly from Cognism.

features.title: Phone-verified mobile data
features.description: Diamond Data uses human researchers to manually confirm mobile numbers rather than algorithmic prediction alone.

features.title: Verified email data
features.description: Contact-level email data intended to reduce bounce rates in outbound campaigns.

features.title: EMEA-focused coverage
features.description: Strong data coverage across UK, DACH, Benelux, Nordics, France, and Spain relative to US-centric providers.

features.title: CRM and sales engagement integrations
features.description: Native connectors for Salesforce, HubSpot, Outreach, Salesloft, Microsoft Dynamics 365, Bullhorn, and Pipedrive.

features.title: Chrome extension / LinkedIn prospecting
features.description: Surfaces verified contact details while browsing LinkedIn Sales Navigator or a company's website.

features.title: Compliance tooling
features.description: GDPR/CCPA compliance, Do Not Call and TPS list scrubbing across roughly 15 territories, ISO 27001 and SOC 2 Type II certification.

features.title: Intent and firmographic data
features.description: Company-level data and buying signals layered on top of contact records for account prioritization.

pros: Strong reputation for data accuracy in European and EMEA markets specifically
Human-verified mobile numbers rather than purely predictive/algorithmic data
Heavy emphasis on GDPR/CCPA compliance and Do Not Call list scrubbing, reducing legal risk for outbound teams
Broad CRM and sales engagement integrations
cons: No public pricing; requires a sales conversation and negotiation to get a quote
Reported contract costs can be significant, with platform fees alone often in the $15,000-$25,000/year range
US data coverage is generally considered less comprehensive than EMEA coverage
Primarily designed for outbound sales teams rather than broader marketing or research use cases

faqs.question: How much does Cognism cost?
faqs.answer: Cognism does not publish pricing. Third-party sources tracking real deals report typical annual contracts ranging from roughly $1,500 to $25,000 depending on tier, seat count, and negotiation; buyers must request a quote directly from Cognism.

faqs.question: Is Cognism GDPR compliant?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cognism markets GDPR and CCPA compliance as a core part of its product, including notifying contacts of database inclusion within GDPR timeframes and regular scrubbing against Do Not Call lists.

faqs.question: What CRMs does Cognism integrate with?
faqs.answer: Cognism has native integrations with Salesforce, HubSpot, Outreach, Salesloft, Microsoft Dynamics 365, Bullhorn, and Pipedrive.

faqs.question: How is Cognism's phone data verified?
faqs.answer: Its Diamond Data tier relies on human researchers who call and manually confirm mobile numbers rather than relying purely on algorithmic prediction, with a reported accuracy around 87%.

faqs.question: Who founded Cognism?
faqs.answer: Cognism was founded by James Isilay (CEO) and Stjepan Buljat (CTO) in 2015, and is headquartered in London.

use_cases.title: Outbound SDR prospecting
use_cases.description: Sales development reps use Cognism to build verified contact lists for cold calling and cold email campaigns, especially into European accounts.

use_cases.title: European GTM expansion
use_cases.description: A US-based company expanding into EMEA uses Cognism for compliant, region-specific contact data that meets GDPR requirements.
use_cases.audience: RevOps and sales leadership expanding into Europe

enrichment.short_description: Cognito Forms is a drag-and-drop online form builder with conditional logic, payment collection, and workflow automation, offering a free plan and paid tiers.
enrichment.seo_title: Cognito Forms: Pricing, Features & Free Plan Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Cognito Forms is a no-code form builder with conditional logic and payments. Compare its Free, Pro, Team, and Enterprise plans and key features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Cognito Forms is an online form-building platform founded on December 10, 2013 by Alain Meier and Jamie Thomas, following several years of custom web development work for clients in Columbia, South Carolina, where the company remains headquartered. It grew out of the founders repeatedly building bespoke data-collection tools for clients and deciding to turn that into a single self-serve platform; the company reports around 47 employees as of 2026. Cognito Forms provides a drag-and-drop, no-code form builder that lets users create and embed custom forms with conditional logic (showing or hiding fields based on prior answers), automated cost calculations, date comparisons, electronic signature capture, and file uploads. It supports accepting payments directly through forms via Stripe, PayPal, or Square, making it commonly used for registrations, applications, and order forms that need to collect money as part of the submission. Data-handling features include entry sharing, data merging, confirmation email management, folder-level permissions, and HIPAA-compliant configurations on higher plans, along with data encryption. It integrates with other software through Zapier, Microsoft Power Automate, and JSON webhooks, letting form submissions trigger workflows in other systems without custom code. Pricing follows a four-tier freemium model. The Individual plan is free forever, with no credit card required, and includes one user, up to 100 form entries per month, 100 MB of storage, and unlimited forms with payment acceptance. The Pro plan costs $24/month billed monthly or $19.20/month billed annually, covering 2 users, 2,000 entries per month, 1 GB of storage, electronic signatures, save-and-resume functionality, and the ability to remove Cognito Forms branding. The Team plan, described as the best-value tier, costs $49/month monthly or $39.20/month annually, covering 5 users plus 5 included guest accounts, 10,000 entries per month, 10 GB of storage, a client portal, workflow and approval features, and data lookups. The Enterprise plan costs $174/month monthly or $129.60/month annually, covering 20 users plus 20 guests, unlimited entries, 100 GB of storage, HIPAA compliance, folder permissions, and single sign-on. All paid plans build on the free tier's unlimited-forms and payment-acceptance foundation while raising entry volume, storage, and collaboration limits. Cognito Forms is best suited for small businesses, nonprofits, schools, and healthcare-adjacent organizations that need more configurable logic and payment handling than a basic contact-form tool provides, without requiring a developer to build custom logic.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cognito Forms is a no-code online form builder founded in 2013 by Alain Meier and Jamie Thomas in Columbia, South Carolina. It grew out of the founders' custom web development work and was built to let non-developers create sophisticated forms with logic, calculations, and payment collection.

The platform serves small businesses, nonprofits, schools, and healthcare organizations that need forms more capable than basic contact forms but without the cost or complexity of building custom software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Forms support conditional logic, automatic cost calculations, date comparisons, electronic signatures, and file uploads, all configured through a drag-and-drop builder without code.

Built-in payment acceptance through Stripe, PayPal, or Square lets organizations collect money directly through registration, order, or application forms, while Zapier, Microsoft Power Automate, and webhook support connect form data to other business systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Cognito Forms offers a free-forever Individual plan (1 user, 100 entries/month, 100 MB storage) alongside three paid tiers: Pro at $19.20-$24/month, Team at $39.20-$49/month, and Enterprise at $129.60-$174/month, with the lower figure reflecting annual billing.
Higher tiers raise entry and storage limits and add capabilities like electronic signatures, a client portal, workflow approvals, HIPAA compliance, and single sign-on.

features.title: Drag-and-drop form builder
features.description: No-code interface for building custom forms with a wide range of field types and layouts.

features.title: Conditional logic
features.description: Show, hide, or require fields dynamically based on a respondent's previous answers.

features.title: Payment collection
features.description: Accept credit card payments directly through forms via Stripe, PayPal, or Square.

features.title: Electronic signatures
features.description: Capture legally usable e-signatures as part of a form submission on Pro and higher plans.

features.title: Workflow and approvals
features.description: Route submissions through approval steps and a client portal on the Team plan and above.

features.title: HIPAA-compliant configuration
features.description: Enterprise plan supports HIPAA-compliant data handling for healthcare-related forms.

features.title: Integrations via Zapier and webhooks
features.description: Connect form submissions to other software using Zapier, Microsoft Power Automate, or JSON webhooks.

pros: Genuinely usable free plan with unlimited forms and payment acceptance, not just a trial
Conditional logic and calculations rival more expensive form tools without requiring code
Built-in payment processing simplifies registration and order forms
HIPAA-compliant configuration available for healthcare and sensitive-data use cases
cons: Entry volume and storage limits scale with price, which can force upgrades for growing organizations
Higher-end collaboration features (client portal, approvals, SSO) are locked to Team and Enterprise tiers
Guest user allowances are capped even on paid plans
Smaller integration ecosystem compared to larger form/survey platforms

faqs.question: Does Cognito Forms have a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Individual plan is free with no credit card required, offering unlimited forms, up to 100 entries per month, 100 MB of storage, and payment acceptance.

faqs.question: Can Cognito Forms accept payments?
faqs.answer: Yes, forms can accept credit card payments through Stripe, PayPal, or Square on any plan, including the free tier.

faqs.question: How much does Cognito Forms cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans range from about $19.20/month (Pro, billed annually) to $129.60/month (Enterprise, billed annually), with monthly billing priced somewhat higher on each tier.

faqs.question: Is Cognito Forms HIPAA compliant?
faqs.answer: HIPAA-compliant configuration is available on the Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: What integrations does Cognito Forms support?
faqs.answer: It integrates with other software via Zapier, Microsoft Power Automate, and JSON webhooks.

faqs.question: Who founded Cognito Forms?
faqs.answer: Cognito Forms was founded in 2013 by Alain Meier and Jamie Thomas in Columbia, South Carolina.

use_cases.title: Event registration with payment
use_cases.description: An organization builds a registration form with conditional pricing logic and collects payment directly through the form.

use_cases.title: Healthcare intake forms
use_cases.description: A clinic uses HIPAA-compliant form configuration to collect patient intake information securely.

enrichment.short_description: Colanode is an open-source, local-first collaboration workspace combining Slack-style chat and Notion-style pages, databases, and files, self-hostable by default.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Colanode is an open-source, local-first workspace combining chat, docs, and databases. Self-host it or use the free beta cloud. See features and setup.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Colanode is an open-source, local-first collaboration workspace created in 2024 (the GitHub repository dates to July 29, 2024) as a self-hostable alternative that combines functionality similar to Slack and Notion in one tool. It was introduced publicly on Hacker News as a "Show HN" project and has since grown to over 5,000 GitHub stars across more than 1,000 commits and 40-plus releases, with the codebase released under the Apache 2.0 open-source license and written primarily in TypeScript. The product bundles four main capabilities: real-time chat for team messaging, rich-text pages for documents and wikis, customizable databases with multiple views (table, kanban, and calendar) for structured data, and file management for storing and sharing files within a workspace. Its defining architectural feature is a local-first design: every change is written to a local SQLite database on the user's device first, and a background sync process pushes changes to the server, so the app remains usable offline and reads happen instantly from local storage rather than waiting on a network round trip. Real-time collaborative editing (for example, multiple people editing the same page simultaneously) is powered by Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs) via the Yjs library, which merges concurrent edits without conflicts. Colanode ships as both a web app (accessible at app.colanode.com) and a desktop application built with Electron, and it can be self-hosted for full control over data. Self-hosting requires PostgreSQL with the pgvector extension, Redis (or a Valkey-compatible alternative), and a file storage backend, which defaults to the local filesystem but also supports S3-compatible storage, Google Cloud Storage, or Azure Blob Storage; documentation describes both a simple single-server setup and a more complex, production-grade distributed deployment. For users who don't want to run their own infrastructure, Colanode also offers hosted cloud servers in the EU and US regions, currently in beta and free to use while the company works out a longer-term monetization strategy; as of mid-2026 there is no finalized public pricing for a paid cloud tier. Because it is fully open source and self-hostable, Colanode is positioned for privacy- and data-ownership-conscious teams, technical organizations that already run their own infrastructure, and individuals or small teams who want a Notion/Slack-style tool without a recurring SaaS subscription or third-party data hosting. It's best evaluated as a young, actively developed project rather than a mature enterprise product: as of 2026 its cloud monetization plan is still being finalized, and adopters should expect a self-hosted or beta-cloud experience rather than a polished managed-service offering with SLAs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Colanode is an open-source, local-first collaboration workspace created in mid-2024 that combines Slack-style chat with Notion-style pages, databases, and file storage in one self-hostable application. It is released under the Apache 2.0 license and has grown to over 5,000 GitHub stars.

Its local-first architecture writes changes to a local SQLite database first and syncs to the server in the background, so the app remains usable offline and reads happen instantly from the local device.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform bundles real-time chat, rich-text pages/wikis, customizable databases with table, kanban, and calendar views, and file management in a single workspace. Real-time collaborative editing is powered by CRDTs via the Yjs library, allowing multiple people to edit the same content simultaneously without conflicts.

It's available as a web app, a desktop app built with Electron, and a self-hosted server. Self-hosting requires PostgreSQL with pgvector, Redis or Valkey, and a storage backend (local filesystem, S3-compatible, Google Cloud Storage, or Azure Blob Storage).

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Colanode itself is free and open source with no license fee to self-host. The company also offers hosted Colanode Cloud servers in the EU and US, currently in a free public beta while a long-term monetization approach is finalized.

As of mid-2026 there is no published paid pricing tier; teams that want guaranteed pricing or support should plan around self-hosting rather than assuming the current free cloud beta will remain free indefinitely.

features.title: Real-time chat
features.description: Slack-style instant messaging channels within a workspace.

features.title: Rich-text pages
features.description: Notion-style documents and wikis with nested pages.

features.title: Customizable databases
features.description: Structured data with table, kanban, and calendar views similar to Notion databases.

features.title: File management
features.description: Store and share files within secure workspaces alongside chat and pages.

features.title: Local-first sync
features.description: Local SQLite storage with background sync keeps the app usable offline and fast to read.

features.title: CRDT-based collaboration
features.description: Yjs-powered conflict-free real-time editing lets multiple users edit the same content simultaneously.

features.title: Self-hosting
features.description: Full self-hosted deployment using PostgreSQL, Redis/Valkey, and a choice of storage backends.

pros: Fully open source (Apache 2.0) with an active GitHub community and frequent releases
Local-first design keeps the app fast and usable offline
Combines chat, docs, databases, and files in one tool instead of several separate apps
Free to self-host with no per-seat licensing cost
cons: Young project (created 2024) without the maturity or ecosystem of established tools like Notion or Slack
Cloud hosting is still in beta with no finalized pricing, so long-term cost is uncertain
Self-hosting requires managing PostgreSQL, Redis/Valkey, and storage infrastructure
Smaller integration and plugin ecosystem compared to mature commercial alternatives

faqs.question: Is Colanode free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Colanode is open source (Apache 2.0) and free to self-host. Its hosted cloud option is also currently free while in beta.

faqs.question: Can Colanode be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes. Self-hosting requires PostgreSQL with the pgvector extension, Redis or Valkey, and a storage backend such as the local filesystem, S3-compatible storage, Google Cloud Storage, or Azure Blob Storage.

faqs.question: What does Colanode replace?
faqs.answer: It's positioned as a combined alternative to Slack (chat) and Notion (pages, wikis, and databases) in a single self-hostable application.

faqs.question: Does Colanode work offline?
faqs.answer: Yes. Its local-first architecture saves changes to a local SQLite database first and syncs to the server in the background, so it remains usable offline.

faqs.question: What license is Colanode released under?
faqs.answer: Colanode is released under the Apache 2.0 open-source license.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious team collaboration
use_cases.description: A team that wants full control over its data self-hosts Colanode to get Slack-and-Notion-style functionality without relying on third-party SaaS storage.

use_cases.title: Offline-capable team workspace
use_cases.description: A distributed or field team relies on Colanode's local-first sync to keep working on chat, pages, and files even without a stable internet connection.

enrichment.short_description: Collabora Online is an open-source, self-hostable office suite for real-time collaborative editing of documents, spreadsheets, and presentations in a browser.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (CODE edition); Business tier from roughly €3/user/month
enrichment.seo_title: Collabora Online: Open-Source Office Suite Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Collabora Online is a self-hosted, open-source alternative to Google Workspace and Microsoft 365. See its features, editions, and pricing tiers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Collabora Online is a browser-based, real-time collaborative office suite built on LibreOffice Online technology and developed by Collabora, a UK open-source consulting company founded in 2005 by Robert McQueen, Philippe Kalaf, and Robert Taylor and headquartered in Cambridge, with an additional office in Montreal. Collabora is one of the largest corporate contributors to the LibreOffice codebase, and Collabora Online packages that engine into a web-based suite covering Writer (word processing), Calc (spreadsheets), Impress (presentations), and Draw (vector graphics), positioned as an open-source alternative to Google Workspace and Microsoft 365. Unlike those proprietary cloud suites, Collabora Online is designed to be self-hosted (or hosted by a third party) rather than run exclusively as Collabora's own SaaS product, and it does not function as a standalone service; it integrates into an existing file-storage platform such as Nextcloud, ownCloud, EGroupware, or Pydio, which supplies the file storage and user management while Collabora Online provides the in-browser editing layer. It supports the OpenDocument format natively and offers strong compatibility with Microsoft Office formats (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx), enabling real-time multi-user editing directly in a browser. For offline and native use, Collabora also ships apps for Android, ChromeOS, iOS, iPadOS, Linux, macOS, and Windows. The product is distributed in two main tracks. Collabora Online Development Edition (CODE) is completely free, released roughly monthly, and recommended for testing, personal use, or small teams, but explicitly not recommended for large-scale production deployments due to its faster-moving, less battle-tested release cycle; it's the version most commonly bundled for free with self-hosted Nextcloud installs. Commercial, production-grade editions are sold through Collabora under subscription pricing, generally billed annually, with reported starting rates around €3 per user per month for on-premise deployments of up to about 99 users, and custom Enterprise-scale pricing for larger organizations; these paid tiers add long-term support, signed security updates, and higher stability and scalability guarantees. Collabora is also GDPR compliant by design, since self-hosting keeps data within an organization's own infrastructure rather than a third-party cloud. Because self-hosted deployments eliminate recurring per-user SaaS fees, Collabora Online can be more cost-effective than Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace at scale for organizations with the technical capacity to run and maintain the infrastructure, particularly public sector bodies, privacy-focused organizations, and companies already running Nextcloud or ownCloud. It is less suited to organizations that want a fully managed, zero-maintenance cloud office suite without any self-hosting involvement.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Collabora Online is a browser-based collaborative office suite built on LibreOffice technology, developed by Collabora, an open-source consulting company founded in 2005 and headquartered in Cambridge, UK. It's positioned as a self-hostable, open-source alternative to Google Workspace and Microsoft 365.

Rather than running as a standalone cloud service, Collabora Online plugs into an existing file-storage platform such as Nextcloud, ownCloud, EGroupware, or Pydio, which handles storage and user accounts while Collabora provides the in-browser editing engine for Writer, Calc, Impress, and Draw documents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: It enables real-time, multi-user collaborative editing of documents, spreadsheets, presentations, and vector graphics directly in a browser, with strong compatibility for both the OpenDocument format and Microsoft Office file formats.

Native apps extend offline access across Android, ChromeOS, iOS, iPadOS, Linux, macOS, and Windows, and because it's self-hosted, organizations keep full control over where document data is stored, supporting GDPR and other data-residency requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Collabora Online Development Edition (CODE) is free and updated roughly monthly, suited to testing, personal use, or small teams, but not recommended for large-scale production due to its faster, less stable release cadence.

Commercial, production-ready editions are sold on an annual subscription, with reported starting rates around €3 per user per month for on-premise deployments up to about 99 users, and custom pricing for larger Enterprise deployments that add long-term support and signed security updates.

features.title: Real-time collaborative editing
features.description: Multiple users can edit the same document, spreadsheet, or presentation simultaneously in a browser.

features.title: Writer, Calc, Impress, Draw
features.description: Full office suite covering word processing, spreadsheets, presentations, and vector graphics.

features.title: OpenDocument and Microsoft Office compatibility
features.description: Native support for ODF plus strong compatibility with .docx, .xlsx, and .pptx files.

features.title: Self-hosted deployment
features.description: Runs on an organization's own infrastructure integrated with Nextcloud, ownCloud, EGroupware, or Pydio.

features.title: Native offline apps
features.description: Dedicated apps for Android, ChromeOS, iOS, iPadOS, Linux, macOS, and Windows.

features.title: CODE free edition
features.description: No-cost development edition suitable for testing, personal use, and small teams.

features.title: GDPR-friendly data residency
features.description: Self-hosting keeps documents within an organization's own infrastructure rather than third-party cloud servers.

pros: Fully self-hostable, giving organizations complete control over document data and residency
Free CODE edition available for testing or small-scale personal use
Strong file format compatibility with both OpenDocument and Microsoft Office formats
Can be more cost-effective than per-user SaaS office suites at scale for organizations that can run their own infrastructure
cons: Requires an existing platform like Nextcloud or ownCloud plus infrastructure to self-host; it isn't a simple standalone signup
Free CODE edition is explicitly not recommended for large-scale production use
Commercial support and stable enterprise editions require a paid subscription
Feature parity with Microsoft 365's most advanced collaboration and AI features is not guaranteed

faqs.question: Is Collabora Online free?
faqs.answer: The Collabora Online Development Edition (CODE) is free, suited to testing, personal use, or small teams. Production-grade, commercially supported editions require a paid subscription.

faqs.question: Can Collabora Online be used without Nextcloud?
faqs.answer: It requires an underlying file-storage and user-management platform such as Nextcloud, ownCloud, EGroupware, or Pydio; it does not run as a fully standalone product.

faqs.question: Does Collabora Online support Microsoft Office file formats?
faqs.answer: Yes, it offers strong compatibility with Microsoft Office formats such as .docx, .xlsx, and .pptx, alongside native OpenDocument support.

faqs.question: How much does Collabora Online cost for businesses?
faqs.answer: Commercial pricing has been reported starting around €3 per user per month for on-premise deployments up to about 99 users, billed annually, with custom pricing for larger Enterprise deployments.

faqs.question: Who develops Collabora Online?
faqs.answer: It's developed by Collabora, a UK-based open-source consulting company founded in 2005 and headquartered in Cambridge, which is also one of the largest contributors to the LibreOffice codebase.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted office suite for a Nextcloud deployment
use_cases.description: An organization already running Nextcloud adds Collabora Online (CODE or a paid edition) to get in-browser document editing without sending files to a third-party cloud.

use_cases.title: Public sector document collaboration
use_cases.description: A government agency with data-residency requirements self-hosts Collabora Online to keep collaborative document editing entirely on infrastructure it controls.

enrichment.short_description: Providence is the free, open-source cataloging engine of CollectiveAccess, used by museums, archives, and libraries to manage and describe digital and physical collections.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Providence is CollectiveAccess's open-source cataloging software for museums and archives. See its features, licensing, and who maintains it.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: CollectiveAccess is free, open-source software for cataloging and publishing museum, archival, and library collections, with the project originating in 2003 in response to a lack of affordable, non-commercial collections management options. It is maintained by Whirl-i-Gig, a small software and consulting company based in Brooklyn, New York, with ongoing contributions from the open-source community. The suite consists of two applications: Providence, the back-end cataloging component, and Pawtucket2, the front-end public-facing web publishing component used to make collections browsable and searchable online. Providence is the core system institutions install and configure to catalog their holdings; it is highly configurable and can support a wide range of metadata standards and content types, including documents, images, audio, video, and 3D objects, without requiring custom programming for most configuration changes. Its feature set includes web-based cataloging forms, batch import and export of records and media, customizable data models, user and role-based access control, and reporting tools. The current major release line is CollectiveAccess 2.0, and the software runs on a PHP/MySQL stack, with recent versions supporting PHP 8.2 and 8.3. It is released under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3), meaning it can be freely downloaded, installed, modified, and shared without licensing fees. CollectiveAccess is used by hundreds of institutions worldwide, spanning national museums, local history consortia, specialized archives, and catalogues raisonnés, including notable implementations such as the Kress Digital Archive at the National Gallery of Art, Chicago Film Archives, and the Samek Art Museum at Bucknell University. Because it is entirely free and open source, there is no vendor subscription fee for the software itself; institutions instead invest in their own hosting and staff time to install, configure, and maintain it, or optionally pay Whirl-i-Gig for professional services such as custom development, data migration, digital preservation consulting, hosting, and public-facing website design and development. Documentation, community forums, live chat support, and a public demonstration sandbox are available to help institutions evaluate and configure the system before committing resources. CollectiveAccess/Providence is best suited for museums, archives, libraries, and research collections that need a flexible, standards-based cataloging system and have some in-house or contracted technical capacity to install and maintain a self-hosted PHP/MySQL application, rather than organizations looking for a fully managed, no-setup SaaS product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CollectiveAccess is free, open-source collections management software that began in 2003 to address the lack of affordable, non-commercial cataloging options for museums and archives. It is maintained by Whirl-i-Gig, a small development and consulting company based in Brooklyn, New York, with community contributions.

The suite has two parts: Providence, the configurable back-end cataloging system institutions use to describe and manage their holdings, and Pawtucket2, the companion front-end for publishing collections online for public browsing and search.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Providence supports a wide range of metadata standards and content types, including documents, images, audio, video, and 3D objects, with web-based cataloging, batch import/export, and customizable data models that don't require custom programming for most configuration.

It runs on a PHP/MySQL stack (current builds support PHP 8.2/8.3), is released under the GPLv3 open-source license, and is used by hundreds of institutions worldwide, from national museums to small local archives.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The software itself is entirely free to download, install, and use under the GPLv3 license, with no subscription fee. Institutions cover their own hosting and staff time, or optionally contract Whirl-i-Gig for custom development, data migration, hosting, or public-website services.
Because pricing for professional services is scoped per project rather than published as fixed tiers, there is no standard public price list beyond the software being free.

features.title: Configurable cataloging
features.description: Highly customizable data models and metadata standards without requiring custom code for most configuration changes.

features.title: Multi-format media support
features.description: Handles documents, images, audio, video, and 3D object records within the same system.

features.title: Batch import and export
features.description: Bulk data and media import/export tools for migrating or updating large collections.

features.title: Public-facing publishing (Pawtucket2)
features.description: Companion application for making cataloged collections searchable and browsable online.

features.title: Role-based access control
features.description: User and role permissions for controlling who can view or edit specific records.

features.title: Open-source licensing
features.description: Released under GPLv3, free to download, modify, and self-host without licensing fees.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing cost
Highly configurable for diverse collection types (museum objects, archival documents, audio/video, 3D)
Long track record, in active use since 2003 by hundreds of institutions
Optional professional services (hosting, migration, development) available through the maintaining company
cons: Requires self-hosting a PHP/MySQL application, which demands in-house or contracted technical capacity
No fully managed, zero-setup SaaS option from the core project
Configuration complexity can be significant for institutions without prior systems experience
Documentation and support are community/vendor-driven rather than a large commercial support organization

faqs.question: Is CollectiveAccess free?
faqs.answer: Yes. CollectiveAccess, including its Providence cataloging component, is free, open-source software released under the GPLv3 license.

faqs.question: Who maintains CollectiveAccess?
faqs.answer: It's maintained by Whirl-i-Gig, a software and consulting company based in Brooklyn, New York, with contributions from the open-source community.

faqs.question: What's the difference between Providence and Pawtucket2?
faqs.answer: Providence is the back-end cataloging application institutions use to manage collection records; Pawtucket2 is the companion front-end for publishing those collections publicly online.

faqs.question: What technology does CollectiveAccess run on?
faqs.answer: It runs on a PHP and MySQL stack; current releases support PHP 8.2 and 8.3.

faqs.question: Who uses CollectiveAccess?
faqs.answer: Hundreds of institutions, including national museums, local history consortia, and specialized archives, such as the Kress Digital Archive at the National Gallery of Art and Chicago Film Archives.

use_cases.title: Museum object cataloging
use_cases.description: A museum installs Providence to catalog its physical collection with detailed, standards-based metadata and manage digitized images of each object.

use_cases.title: Public archive with online browsing
use_cases.description: An archive combines Providence for internal cataloging with Pawtucket2 to publish a searchable, browsable version of its collection to the public.
